figures.caption: Figure 1: Our post-hoc framework to explain (embed 1 given concepts, cluster 2 and name found parents 3 ), verify 4 , and align 5 the semantic hierarchy induced by a VLM in its embedding space.
Figure 3: Faithfulness (top) versus plausibility (bottom) results for different models and backbones, averaged over 3 datasets and 3 leaf encoding types (image, text, both).

Table 14: Results of the ontology manipulation: Hyperparameter search based on leaf swapping, averaged over 10 swaps and 3 runs (Validation task), adaptation to an ontology extracted from the text embeddings (Modality alignment) and to three SUMO ontologies (Ontological commitment); both averaged over 5 runs.

Figure 13: For the flava-full VLM model we achieved to learn a transformation from the image embedding space to the text embedding space which achieves full alignment of image and text ontology (except for symmetry).
Table 15: Simple Linear Model to approximate the denoted embedding transformation, all runs are averaged over five seeds.
Figure 14: UMAP visualization of the original embedding space based on the test dataset

Table 12: Detailed results on how much early stopping improves upon predicting correct (super-)categories: Tree walking distances (↓) between predicted node and the last correct node (LCA of the ground truth with the leaf predicted by tree traversal); averaged over models.

Figure 5: Comparing text embedding strategies: Top 1 zero-shot accuracy per text embedding strategy on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet and for different leaf embedding strategies (averaged). Top: averaged over models, bottom: per model.
Figure 10: Comparison of the two SUMO validity scores. If not stated otherwise, averaged over models, datasets, leaf and text embedding strategies.

Table 11: Hypothesis test: Zero-shot > tree inference accuracy. Results of the one-sided t-tests testing against the 0-hypothesis that top-1 zero-shot accuracy is not different from tree inference / soft tree inference accuracy, instead of being greater. Each test includes more than 10 experiments (different datasets, models, and leaf embedding strategies).

Table 10: Hypothesis tests: Zero-shot correlated. Results of the two-sided Person Correlation t-test testing for correlation of top-1 zero-shot accuracy (top) respectively faithfulness (bottom) with other metrics (excluding CUB dataset). Experiments vary over 13 models, 3 datasets, and 3 leaf embedding methods. Note that results also proof the respective one-sided case.

Figure 8: Explaining the faithfulness gap: Comparison of faithfulness metrics, in particular soft tree inference accuracy, tree inference accuracy, and zero-shot accuracy, averaged over models, datasets, text embedding strategies WordNet & WordNet+LLM, and leaf embeddings trategies. Top: by model; bottom: by dataset and leaf embedding strategy.

Figure 11: Comparison of zero-shot performance and faithfulness for textual leaf concepts and different augmenters (top); comparison of hierarchical consistency for different augmenters (bottom); averaged over datasets, models, (and modalities).
Table 9: Faithfulness metrics by encoder and dataset, averaged over models.
Figure 7: Comparing leaf embedding strategies: Faithfulness metrics averaged across datasets and models.
Table 8: Faithfulness metrics by dataset in percent, averaged over models and encoders.
Table 6: Plausibility (Hierarchical Consistency) and faithfulness metrics per leaf encoding strategy and per dataset, averaged over models.
Figure 15: UMAP visualization after applying the learned transformation to the embedding space of the test dataset
Figure 12: Empirical approximation of the expected normalized UTED across randomly generated binary trees with identical leaf sets. Results based on 200 runs for varying numbers of leaf nodes.
Figure 6: Comparison of datasets: Faithfulness only per dataset and model averaged over text embedding strategies (WordNet, WordNet+LLM).
